questions.text: I rarely sense the invisible boundaries that others seem to navigate naturally in social spaces.
I hold the view that the seriousness with which I respond to suffering is the most reliable way I have to check whether I am living according to my stated values.
I feel that delayed gratification is a skill worth cultivating.
I hold the view that most social signals are overinterpreted by people who look for them.
I start my day open to whatever feels most compelling rather than consulting a schedule.
I am willing to shoulder the burden of an unpopular choice.
I feel responsible for making sure everyone is part of what happens.
I pick up on the implicit rules of a social gathering without anyone explaining them to me.
I sometimes wish I could turn down the volume on my emotional responses.
I find that my most productive periods are when I am singularly focused on my own objectives.
I find that my emotional responses are like a dimmer switch set to moderate rather than full brightness.
I naturally defer to others in informal group settings.
I find that arguments or confrontations don't leave me shaken afterward.
I tend to volunteer for the most thankless tasks because someone has to do them.
I find that approaching human tragedy as a problem to be analyzed and solved is the most productive use of my capabilities.
I can work effectively in chaotic environments that would distract most people.
I routinely set ambitious goals for myself and dedicate significant effort to reaching them.
I can listen to someone describe their suffering and maintain complete emotional equilibrium.
